problem_text: Determine whether integral_0^1 1/sqrt(x) dx converges, and if so find its value.

content.label: Improper: unbounded integrand at 0
content.applies_when: the integrand blows up at x=0, so the integral is improper at the lower limit and defined as a limit t->0+.

content.concept: Improper integral as a one-sided limit
content.meaning: integral_0^b f dx = lim_{t->0+} integral_t^b f dx when f is unbounded at 0.

content.label: note the integrand x^-1/2 is unbounded at x=0 and rewrite integral_0^1 = lim_{t->0+} integral_t^1
content.action: note the integrand x^-1/2 is unbounded at x=0 and rewrite integral_0^1 = lim_{t->0+} integral_t^1
content.purpose: convert the improper integral into a one-sided limit

content.label: the antiderivative of x^-1/2 is 2 sqrt(x), so integral_t^1 = 2 - 2 sqrt(t)
content.action: the antiderivative of x^-1/2 is 2 sqrt(x), so integral_t^1 = 2 - 2 sqrt(t)
content.purpose: evaluate the proper integral from the cutoff t to 1

content.label: let t -> 0+: sqrt(t) -> 0, so the limit is 2
content.action: let t -> 0+: sqrt(t) -> 0, so the limit is 2
content.purpose: resolve the limit to find the value of the improper integral

content.label: p-integral at 0 converges for p<1
content.applies_when: the integral of x^-p near 0 has p = 1/2 < 1 (the rule is reversed relative to the tail case)
